extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r3, whose condition (qt_prolongation AND heart_failure AND anticoagulated AND low_ejection_fraction) is a subset/superset that includes qt_prolongation. rec_drug_b is set true only by r2, whose condition is qt_prolongation alone. Because r3's guard implies r2's guard, any input that fires r3 necessarily also fires r2. The two rules write to different outputs (rec_drug_a vs rec_drug_b), so the per-output priority/tie-breaking resolution never causes one to suppress the other; both outputs are set true independently. Setting qt_prolongation=heart_failure=anticoagulated=low_ejection_fraction=true makes r3 fire (rec_drug_a=true) and r2 fire (rec_drug_b=true), so rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b are jointly true, violating the mutual-exclusion property. The other inputs only affect rec_drug_c and are set false. Property is violated.
